metadata.summary: Macro recording and metaprogramming in Python

documentation:
[![License BSD-3](https://img.shields.io/pypi/l/macro-kit.svg?color=green)](https://github.com/hanjinliu/macro-kit/blob/main/LICENSE)
[![Python package index download statistics](https://img.shields.io/pypi/dm/macro-kit.svg)](https://pypistats.org/packages/macro-kit)
[![PyPI version](https://badge.fury.io/py/macro-kit.svg)](https://badge.fury.io/py/macro-kit)
[![Conda version](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/macro-kit/badges/version.svg)](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/macro-kit/badges/version.svg)

# macro-kit

`macro-kit` is a package for efficient macro recording and metaprogramming in Python using abstract syntax tree (AST).

The design of AST in this package is strongly inspired by [Julia metaprogramming](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/metaprogramming/). Similar methods are also implemented in builtin `ast` module but `macro-kit` (Julia-style metaprogramming) is more convenient in code operation and also focused on the macro generation and customization.


## Installation

- use pip

```
pip install macro-kit -U
```

- from source

```
pip install git+https://github.com/hanjinliu/macro-kit
```

## Examples

1. Define a macro-recordable function

```python
from macrokit import Macro, Expr, Symbol
macro = Macro()

@macro.record
def str_add(a, b):
    return str(a) + str(b)

val0 = str_add(1, 2)
val1 = str_add(val0, "xyz")
macro
```
```
[Out]
var0x24fdc2d1530 = str_add(1, 2)
var0x24fdc211df0 = str_add(var0x24fdc2d1530, 'xyz')
```

Use `format` method to rename variable names.

```python
# substitute identifiers of variables
# var0x24fdc2d1530 -> x
macro.format([(val0, "x")])
```
```
[Out]
x = str_add(1, 2)
var0x24fdc211df0 = str_add(x, 'xyz')
```

`format` also support substitution with more complicated expressions.

```python
# substitute to _dict["key"]
expr = Expr(head="getitem", args=[Symbol("_dict"), "key"])
macro.format([(val0, expr)])
```
```
[Out]
_dict['key'] = str_add(1, 2)
var0x24fdc211df0 = str_add(_dict['key'], 'xyz')
```

2. Record class

```python
macro = Macro()

@macro.record
class C:
    def __init__(self, val: int):
        self.value = val

    @property
    def value(self):
        return self._value

    @value.setter
    def value(self, new_value: int):
        if not isinstance(new_value, int):
            raise TypeError("new_value must be an integer.")
        self._value = new_value

    def show(self):
        print(self._value)

c = C(1)
c.value = 5
c.value = -10
c.show()
```
```
[Out]
-10
```

Note that value assignments are not recorded in duplicate.

```python
macro.format([(c, "ins")])
```
```
[Out]
ins = C(1)
ins.value = -10
var0x7ffed09d2cd8 = ins.show()
```

`eval` can evaluate macro.

```python
macro.eval({"C": C})
```
```
[Out]
-10
```

3. Record module

```python
import numpy as np
macro = Macro()
np = macro.record(np)  # macro-recordable numpy

arr = np.random.random(30)
mean = np.mean(arr)

macro
```
```
[Out]
var0x2a0a2864090 = numpy.random.random(30)
var0x2a0a40daef0 = numpy.mean(var0x2a0a2864090)
```

Recorded module is stored in `Symbol` so you can safely `eval` the macro without passing the module object as the global variables.

```python
macro.eval()  # this works
```

4. String parsing

`parse` calls `ast.parse` inside so that you can safely make `Expr` from string.

```python
from macrokit import parse

expr = parse("result = f(0, l[2:8])")
expr
```
```
[Out]
:(result = f(0, l[slice(2, 8, None)]))
```
```python
print(expr.dump())
```
```
[Out]
head: assign
args:
 0: result
 1: head: call
    args:
     0: f
     1: 0
     2: head: getitem
        args:
         0: l
         1: slice(2, 8, None)
```

functions.name: macrokit.type_map.register_type
documentation:
Define a dispatcher for macro recording.

```pycon
>>> register_type(np.ndarray, lambda arr: str(arr.tolist()))
```

or

```pycon
>>> @register_type(np.ndarray)
>>> def _(arr):
...     return str(arr.tolist())
```

or if you defined a new type

```pycon
>>> @register_type(lambda t: t.name)
>>> class T:
...     ...
```

methods.name: macrokit.mock.Mock.not_
documentation: Return a Mock with expression 'not mock'.

Helper class for easier Expr object handling.

Instead of writing like `Expr("getattr", [a, b])`, Mock object can do it.

```pycon
>>> mock = Mock("a")
>>> mock.b  # Mock<a.b>
>>> mock.method(arg=10)  # Mock<a.method(arg=10)>
```

functions.name: macrokit.ipython.magic.register_magic
documentation:
Make a magic command more like Julia's macro system.

Instead of using string, you can register a magic that uses Expr as the
input and return a modified Expr. It is usually easier and safer to
execute metaprogramming this way.

# Parameters

func

: Function that will used as a magic command.

# Returns

Callable

: Registered function itself.

# Examples

```python
@register_magic
def print_code(expr):
    print(expr)
    return expr
```

The `print_code` magic is registered as an ipython magic.

```python
%print_code a = 1
```

```python
%%print_code
def func(a):
    return a + 1
```

A wrapper that convert an object to a macro-recordable one.

Parameters
----------

    expression or an expression with the last returned value as inputs.

functions.name: macrokit.expression.symbol
documentation:
Make a proper Symbol or Expr instance from any objects.

Unlike Symbol(...) constructor, which directly make a Symbol from a string, this
function checks input type and determine the optimal string to represent the
object. Especially, Symbol("xyz") will return `:xyz` while symbol("xyz") will
return `:'xyz'`.

# Parameters

obj

: Any object from which a Symbol will be created.

constant

: If true, object is interpreted as a constant like 1 or "a". Otherwise object is
  converted to a variable that named with its ID.

# Returns

Symbol or Expr

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.at
documentation:
Easier way of tandem get-item.

Helper function to avoid `expr.args[0].args[0] ...`. Also, exception
descriptions during this function call. `expr.at(i, j, k)` is equivalent
to `expr.args[i].args[j].args[k]`.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.eval
documentation:
Evaluate or execute macro as an Python script.

Either `eval` or `exec` will get called, which determined by its header.
Calling this function is much safer than those not-recommended usage of
`eval` or `exec`.

# Parameters

\_globals

: Mapping from global variable symbols to their values.

\_locals

: Updated variable namespace. Will be a mapping from symbols to values.

documentation:

```pycon
>>> expr = parse("f(1, 2, x=3)")
>>> expr.eval_call_args()  # (1, 2), {"x": 3}
```

ns

documentation: Determine if an expression is in the form of `setitem(obj, key value)`.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.iter_args
documentation: Recursively iterate along all the arguments.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.iter_expr
documentation:
Recursively iterate over all the nested Expr, until reaching to non-nested Expr.

This method is useful in macro generation.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.split_getattr
documentation:
Split an expression into a list of get-attribute symbols.

```pycon
>>> expr = parse("a.b.c.d")
>>> expr.split_getattr()  # [:a, :b, :c, :d]
```

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.split_getitem
documentation:
Split an expression into a list of get-item symbols/strings.

```pycon
>>> expr = parse("a['b']['c']['d']")
>>> expr.split_getitem()  # [:a, :'b', :'c', :'d']
```

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.from_callble
documentation: Create function expression from the function itself.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.format
documentation:
Format expressions in the macro.

Just like formatting method of string, this function can replace certain symbols
to others.

# Parameters

mapping

: Mapping from objects to symbols or expressions. Keys will be converted to
  symbol. For instance, if you used `arr`, a numpy.ndarray as an input of an
  macro-recordable function, that input will appear like 'var0x1...'. By
  calling `format([(arr, "X")])` then 'var0x1...' will be substituted to
  'X'.

inplace

: Expression will be overwritten if true.

# Returns

Expression

: Formatted expression.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.iter_lines
documentation: Iterate over all the lines in the expression.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.iter_getattr
documentation: Iterate over all the get-attribute expression.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.iter_call
documentation: Iterate over all the function call.

methods.name: macrokit.expression.Expr.iter_call_args
documentation: Iterate over all the function call and its split.
An expression object for metaprogramming.
